cuisine: Malaysian Chinese — Klang Valley; available at Singapore BKT specialty restaurants
food_group: Hawker Soup — Herbal Pork Rib Soup
food_subgroup: Dark herbal broth with dong gui (Angelica sinensis), dang shen (Codonopsis), wolfberry, dark soy

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Klang/Malaysian herbal-style Bak Kut Teh as a prepared hawker dish. Closest parent would be a generic pork rib soup entry, which is not yet represented in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pork rib soup, dark herbal broth (closest parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code for Klang-style BKT)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0JKG used as closest parent for pork-based soup; no exact code for Malaysian herbal BKT variant.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for this specific prepared hawker dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One claypot serving (~600g) contains approximately 200g pork ribs with bone, 350ml dark herbal broth, plus wolfberries and herbs, yielding approximately 540 kcal, 36g protein, 31g fat, and 20g carbohydrate with ~850mg sodium.

nutrient_source.title: Bak Kut Teh Klang Herbal — HPB Singapore Foodo + Component Calculation
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore — Foodo database; supplemented by IMR Malaysia MyFCD and FAO/INFOODS SEA for herbal component estimation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo used as primary anchor for pork rib soup base values. Herbal components (dong gui, dang shen, wolfberry) estimated via component calculation referencing IMR Malaysia MyFCD and FAO/INFOODS SEA regional composition data. Dark soy contribution to sodium and carbohydrate estimated from HPB condiment entries. Values represent the combined dish per 100g of total claypot weight (~600g total).

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index estimation for pork rib herbal soup — analogous to low-GI meat-based soups with minimal starch; wolfberry and dark soy contributions assessed against University of Sydney GI Database entries for legume-based and soy-based foods
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate); peer-reviewed GI literature for soy-based broths
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Klang-style herbal BKT. GI ~45 estimated by analogy: the dish is predominantly protein and fat (pork ribs, collagen-rich broth) with low net carbohydrate (~3.3g/100g). The dark soy sauce contributes a small starch-thickening effect. Wolfberry adds minimal natural sugars at the portion used. The herbal bitterness (dong gui, dang shen) does not materially affect GI. Analogous foods from the University of Sydney GI Database: pork-based soups and stews with minimal starch additions score GI 35–50. GI 45 is a conservative mid-range estimate. GL per claypot (600g, ~20g carbohydrate): GL = 45 × 20 / 100 = 9.0; input record states 7.65 (based on ~17g net available carbohydrate after fibre deduction), which is consistent and retained.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from dark soy-based broth (~850mg per claypot, ~43% of WHO 2,000mg daily limit before dipping sauce)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit consumption to one claypot serving and avoid the dipping soy sauce, as total sodium with dipping sauce may reach 1,050–1,250mg per meal; request reduced-soy broth where possible and do not drink the full broth.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~45) and low glycemic load (~8–9 per claypot); minimal net carbohydrate from dark soy and wolfberry
type2_diabetes.guidance: Klang-style herbal BKT is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management given its low GI (~45) and low GL (~8–9 per claypot); wolfberry contributes approximately 4g natural fruit sugars per serving which is modest, but patients on insulin or sulfonylureas should account for total carbohydrate (~20g per claypot) in their meal plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork ribs (~12.6g per claypot) and moderate cholesterol (~168mg per claypot); partially offset by collagen-rich broth and herbal anti-inflammatory components
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume Klang-style herbal BKT in moderation; the saturated fat content (~12.6g per claypot, ~63% of AHA daily limit) warrants portion control, and the sodium load (~850mg before dipping sauce) requires careful management in those with concurrent hypertension; dong gui (Angelica sinensis) has mild anticoagulant properties and patients on warfarin or antiplatelet therapy should consult their physician before regular consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Dong gui (Angelica sinensis) has mild anticoagulant and phytoestrogen activity; interaction with most RA medications is not clinically significant
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: For most RA patients on methotrexate, leflunomide, or biologics, Klang-style herbal BKT is acceptable; however, RA patients concurrently on warfarin should exercise caution due to dong gui's anticoagulant properties, and those with hormone-sensitive comorbidities should note the phytoestrogen activity of dong gui.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bak Kut Teh in the Klang/Malaysian herbal style is a slow-simmered pork rib soup cooked in a claypot with a rich, dark, medicinal herbal broth. Unlike the lighter, peppery Teochew/Singapore style, the Klang variant uses a complex blend of TCM herbs including dong gui (Angelica sinensis 当归), dang shen (Codonopsis pilosula 党参), wolfberry (goji berry), dark soy sauce, garlic, star anise, cinnamon, and cloves, producing a deeply aromatic, slightly sweet-bitter, and umami-rich broth with a dark mahogany colour. The pork ribs are fall-off-the-bone tender after prolonged simmering. One claypot (~600g) serves two portions and contains approximately 540 kcal, 36g protein, 31g fat (12.6g saturated), and 20g carbohydrate with ~850mg sodium — notably higher in sodium than Teochew BKT (~700mg) due to the dark soy base. Glycemic index is approximately 45 (low), with a glycemic load of ~8–9 per claypot, making it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management. CRITICAL CLINICAL NOTE: Dong gui (Angelica sinensis) has documented mild anticoagulant activity and phytoestrogen properties — patients on warfarin should exercise caution, and those with ER+ breast cancer or hormone-sensitive conditions should avoid regular consumption. Wolfberry contributes zeaxanthin and lutein beneficial for eye health. The dish is dairy-free and contains soy allergen. Originating from Klang, Selangor, Malaysia — the birthplace of BKT — this style is available in Singapore at specialty restaurants such as Founder BKT on Rangoon Road. Source: HPB Singapore Foodo + component calculation. Confidence: medium.
common_user_queries: klang bkt vs teochew bkt nutrition
malaysian herbal pork rib soup calories

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived from HPB Singapore Foodo as primary anchor for pork rib soup base, supplemented by component calculation for herbal ingredients (dong gui, dang shen, wolfberry) using IMR Malaysia MyFCD and FAO/INFOODS SEA regional data. No single approved source provides an exact match for Klang-style herbal BKT as a complete dish. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this traditional herbal pork rib soup preparation is not available in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases as of 2026-04-14. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the combined claypot dish (broth + ribs + herbs) is not reported as a single value in any approved source consulted. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible for traditional pork rib soup preparation with no hydrogenated fats). All core macros (protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) are non-zero and represent the combined dish per 100g of total claypot weight. Dong gui (Angelica sinensis) anticoagulant and phytoestrogen interactions are clinically documented and flagged in health_context. Sodium per claypot (~850mg) is notably higher than Teochew-style BKT (~700mg) due to dark soy base; total with dipping sauce may reach 1,050–1,250mg. Wolfberry contributes approximately 4g natural fruit sugars per claypot serving. FoodEx2 code A0JKG used as closest parent; no exact code exists for Malaysian herbal BKT. This dish is not halal-certified as it contains pork.
